Title: OneDisplay
Description: You're using a MacBook with an external monitor. You want one screen. The big one.But macOS doesn't let you disable the built-in display without closing the lid.So you're stuck choosing between:Clamshell mode. Lid closed, charger always needed, worse airflow.Brightness to zero. Screen is still active, apps still open there.Terminal hacks. Require a reboot to undo.There had to be a better way.OneDisplay fixes this with one click.Click the menu bar icon and your MacBook display completely disconnects from macOS. Not just dark, gone. Only your external monitor stays on. Your keyboard, trackpad and camera all keep working perfectly. Click again to bring it back.Here is something most people don't realize. M1 and M2 MacBooks officially only support one external monitor. But when you disconnect the built-in display, macOS sees a free display slot. Plug in a second external monitor and suddenly your M1 or M2 is running two external monitors. Something Apple said was never possible.Who this is for:MacBook users with a desk setup and an external monitor. Developers, designers, and creators who want a clean single screen. M1 and M2 users who want to run two external monitors. Anyone tired of apps opening on the wrong display.What makes it different:One click on/off from the menu bar. Zero background processes when not in use. Does one thing perfectly. Works on Apple Silicon M1, M2, M3 and M4. Requires macOS Ventura or later.$9. One-time. Yours forever.If it doesn't work for your setup, email me and I'll refund you. No questions asked.That's the missing macOS feature. Finally.

Title: Tracing PostgreSQL Using eBPF and Hardware Breakpoints
Description: Hardware breakpoints can trigger eBPF programs when specific memory addresses are accessed, leveraging CPU hardware support for low overhead. By utilizing these hardware breakpoints, we can efficiently monitor PostgreSQL’s internal variable updates, such as transaction ID generation and OID assignment. In this post, we will discuss what hardware breakpoints are, whether they have less overhead than uprobes, and how to answer questions like “How many transactions are being executed per second?” or “Which backend is consuming the most OIDs?” with bpftrace.

Title: The Final Form of Software Development
Description: What if the final form of software development was just watching code and proof popping up while you sip a drink? Letting AI agents write assembly directly alongside Lean proofs sidesteps the whole compiler-trust problem. With a peek at real EVM 256-bit addition code and its specification, you'll see why the assembly + Lean paradigm is final in both the historical and category theoretic sense.

Title: From Today, Software Engineering is Dead
Description: When cameras were first introduced, French painter Paul Delaroche reportedly opined “From today, painting is dead.” That was nearly two hundred years ago, and painting is doing just fine. But the nature of painting changed. Photography took over the work of faithful reproduction, and painting became something else: more expressive, more conceptual, more human. I think something similar is happening with code.

Title: The cuda-oxide Book — cuda-oxide
Description: cuda-oxide is an experimental Rust-to-CUDA compiler that lets you write (SIMT) GPU kernels in safe(ish), idiomatic Rust. It compiles standard Rust code directly to PTX — no DSLs, no foreign language bindings, just Rust.
